Council, this case asks a crisp, dispositive question: Did Polymarket, before January 1, 2027 at 00:00 Pacific Standard Time (i.e., before 08:00 UTC on January 1, 2027), launch an official fungible token, and publicly communicate that launch through its own official channels? Our theory is yes. Under the preponderance standard, we will show it is more likely than not that Polymarket released an ERC‑20–style (or equivalent) fungible token that it itself endorsed and announced.

What the dispute is really about
- “Official fungible token”: a transferable, fungible cryptoasset (e.g., ERC‑20 or equivalent) that Polymarket itself created or authorized as its platform token, not a per‑market outcome token, not an NFT, not a non‑transferable or off‑chain points balance, and not a third‑party community coin.
- “Officially released by Polymarket and publicly communicated through official channels”: Polymarket (or a Polymarket‑controlled foundation or corporate entity) must have (1) launched or authorized the token contract and (2) communicated that fact via Polymarket’s website, blog, documentation, press page, email newsletter, verified social media, or official community announcements under its control.
- Exclusions the Council must enforce: (a) NFTs; (b) promotional giveaways or one‑off marketing coupons; (c) tokenized point systems whose primary nature is points rather than a platform token; and (d) tokens launched principally “in collaboration with” other projects or communities such that they are not Polymarket’s own platform token.

Decisive factual questions
1) Existence and identity: Is there a specific fungible token contract asserted to be Polymarket’s platform token, with a definitive name/ticker and on‑chain address?
2) Issuer control/authorization: Does reliable evidence link the token’s deployer, initial mint, or admin keys to Polymarket or its controlled entities (as opposed to an unrelated third party)?
3) Official communication before the deadline: Did an official Polymarket channel, with verifiable timestamps, identify the token (by name/ticker and ideally contract address or canonical link) before 00:00 PST on January 1, 2027? Time‑zone handling must be explicit: the cutoff is 2027‑01‑01 00:00 PST.
4) Nature of the asset: Does the token function as a general‑purpose, transferable fungible asset for the platform, rather than an outcome share, NFT, coupon, or points derivative? Is it independent of a collaborative co‑branding that would remove it from the scope of “official Polymarket token”?
5) Distribution mechanics and messaging: Was any initial distribution characterized as a token launch, not merely a promotional reward or point redemption that the proposition excludes?

Proof that will satisfy the burden
- Official‑channel communications: Posts or pages on Polymarket’s primary domain(s), official blog, documentation/FAQ, press room, verified social accounts, or official community announcements under Polymarket’s control, each with preserved timestamps prior to the deadline. Prefer materials that expressly name the token and, ideally, state or link to the contract address.
- On‑chain corroboration: A verified token contract with creation and first‑mint timestamps before the deadline; transactions originating from an address that independent official channels identify as controlled by Polymarket; or cryptographic signatures/messages published by Polymarket linking its identity to the token address.
- Archival and authenticity: Independent web archives or platform‑native timestamping to fix the timing before 2027‑01‑01 00:00 PST; consistent cross‑references between on‑chain data and official announcements.

What would defeat our claim
- Absence of any official Polymarket communication identifying a platform token before the cutoff.
- Evidence that the only relevant assets were: (a) per‑market outcome tokens; (b) NFTs; (c) points or non‑transferable balances; or (d) a token principally launched by/with another project such that it is not Polymarket’s own platform token.
- A clear, dated official disavowal contemporaneous with the period that Polymarket had no token prior to the deadline.

Anticipated defenses and our responses
- “Outcome shares are fungible tokens.” They are fungible within a market but are not the platform’s official token; they lack the platform‑wide identity and purpose contemplated by the proposition. We will focus on whether Polymarket branded and announced a single platform token.
- “A community or partner issued a ‘Polymarket’ coin.” That is excluded absent Polymarket’s own authorization and official communication.
- “It was only a points redemption.” The proposition excludes tokenized point systems; we will examine the stated purpose and branding to distinguish a platform token from a points wrapper.

Forward‑looking plan for evidence collection
- Identify and preserve Polymarket’s official domains and verified social handles; capture dated announcements naming the token and, if available, listing the contract address.
- Retrieve on‑chain records for the asserted contract(s): creation block/time, deployer, initial holders, admin roles, and any provenance linking to Polymarket‑controlled wallets.
- Correlate timestamps across sources, converting all times to PST to enforce the 2027‑01‑01 00:00 cutoff.
- Compile any contrary official statements to fairly test the exclusion criteria and timing.

Under the preponderance standard, this case will be decided by whether the official communications and on‑chain provenance, taken together, make it more likely than not that Polymarket launched and publicly announced its own fungible platform token before the stated cutoff. That is the showing we intend to make.

Council, the proposition asks you to find that, before 00:00 PST on January 1, 2027 (UTC−8), Polymarket launched an official fungible token and publicly communicated that launch through its own official channels. Under a preponderance standard, the plaintiff must make that entire chain of facts more likely than not. Our theory is that no such qualifying launch and announcement occurred within the period, and that gaps or ambiguities at any element are dispositive against the proposition.

What is genuinely in dispute
- "Official fungible token": a transferable, platform‑level fungible cryptoasset attributable to Polymarket itself, not a per‑market outcome share, not an NFT, not a points wrapper, and not a third‑party or community coin using the brand.
- "Launched": actual mainnet availability of the token under Polymarket’s authority, not a tease, testnet artifact, private pilot, or exchange/partner initiative.
- "Officially released and publicly communicated": authored or controlled Polymarket channels (primary domains, blog/docs, verified social accounts, official community servers) clearly tying Polymarket to the token’s identity and contract lineage before the cutoff. Third‑party press, listings, or rumors do not satisfy this prong.
- Exclusions must be enforced: NFTs, promotional credits, tokenized point systems, and partner‑led launches are out.

Decisive factual questions
1) Token identity: Is there a single canonical token (name/ticker and contract address) asserted as Polymarket’s platform token?
2) Issuer control: Do the deployer/admin roles trace to Polymarket or an entity it controls, as opposed to an unrelated deployer or partner?
3) Public communication timing: Did a Polymarket‑controlled channel, before the PST cutoff, state that Polymarket launched this token and identify it sufficiently (name/ticker and, ideally, contract/reference page)?
4) Nature and scope: Is it platform‑wide and transferable, distinct from outcome shares, NFTs, coupons, or points/loyalty balances?
5) Independence: Was the launch Polymarket’s own, rather than principally a collaboration such that the token is not Polymarket’s platform token?
6) Multichain/wrappers: If bridges or wrappers exist, is there a canonical origin contract and is Polymarket the authoritative issuer?

Proof that would satisfy the burden (plaintiff’s path)
- An official Polymarket announcement before the cutoff that identifies the token and links to a canonical page or contract; plus on‑chain provenance showing Polymarket control over deploy/admin.
- Consistent timestamps normalized to PST, with archival or platform‑native proofs.

Proof that defeats the claim (defense path)
- Absence of any pre‑cutoff official announcement tying Polymarket to a platform‑level token.
- Evidence that the only assets in the period were per‑market outcome tokens, NFTs, promotional/loyalty instruments, or third‑party/community coins.
- On‑chain records showing deploy/admin controlled by non‑Polymarket entities, or only partner‑led launches.
- Announcements (if any) that are ambiguous, post‑cutoff, forward‑looking (intent to launch), or fail to identify a canonical token/contract.

Forward‑looking evidence plan
- Enumerate Polymarket’s official domains and verified social handles; collect all pre‑cutoff posts/pages referencing any platform token; preserve timestamps and convert to PST.
- Map candidate token contracts across chains; extract creation blocks, mint events, admin rights, and links (if any) to Polymarket‑controlled wallets.
- Check for disclaimers or statements distinguishing outcome shares/points from a platform token.
- Identify any partner/co‑branded launches and evaluate them under the explicit exclusions.

Under preponderance, the plaintiff must present clear, dated, official communications linked to verifiable on‑chain control. If any essential link is missing—official attribution, canonical contract, timing before the PST cutoff, or platform‑level scope—the proposition is not proven.

PX-2 Primary evidence summary: pUSD docs, Contracts address, Changelog timing, Polygonscan corroboration

payload.text:
Council,

Under a preponderance standard, the proposition is satisfied if it is more likely than not that, before January 1, 2027 at 00:00 PST, Polymarket (i) launched a fungible token it officially authorized and (ii) publicly communicated that launch on its own channels, with exclusions enforced (no NFTs, promotions, points, or partner/community coins).

What the record shows (official channels and on‑chain corroboration)
1) Token identity and nature (fungible, transferable, platform‑wide):
- Polymarket’s own documentation describes “Polymarket USD (pUSD)” as the collateral token used for all trading on Polymarket, implemented as a standard ERC‑20 on Polygon, transferable, with 6 decimals, and backed by USDC. This is a fungible cryptoasset that operates platform‑wide rather than as per‑market outcome shares. See Primary Evidence ¶1; Official‑Source Record (pUSD concept page).

2) Issuer authorization and canonical contract:
- Polymarket’s Contracts page states it is the single source of truth for Polymarket contract addresses and, under Collateral Contracts, lists “pUSD — CollateralToken (proxy)” at 0xC011a7E12a19f7B1f670d46F03B03f3342E82DFB. That is a direct, on‑domain attribution by Polymarket to a specific token address, evidencing Polymarket’s authority over the contract lineage. See Primary Evidence ¶2; Official‑Source Record (Contracts page).

3) Public communication and timing before the cutoff:
- Polymarket’s Changelog and V2 Migration pages, on Polymarket’s own docs site, state that Polymarket shipped a coordinated exchange upgrade that includes a new collateral token, pUSD, and that CLOB V2 went live on April 28, 2026. The Changelog specifies the go‑live at approximately 11:00 UTC on April 28, 2026, which is 04:00 Pacific Time (UTC−7) on April 28, 2026—well before the January 1, 2027 00:00 PST cutoff. See Primary Evidence ¶¶3–4; Official‑Source Record (Changelog; V2 Migration page).
- Corroboration: Polygonscan shows “Polymarket: pUSD Token” at the same address, consistent with the Contracts page. This is secondary to the official attribution but supports mainnet reality. See Primary Evidence ¶5.

Exclusions are satisfied (why this counts and what it is not)
- Not an NFT: pUSD is an ERC‑20 fungible token.
- Not a promotional offering or coupon: it is the platform’s standard collateral for all trading, not a time‑limited give‑away.
- Not a tokenized point system: the docs characterize it as a standard ERC‑20 backed by USDC; it is transferable and used as trading collateral, not a loyalty balance.
- Not a partner/community launch: the announcement and canonical address come from Polymarket’s official docs, not a third party; there is no co‑branding that would recharacterize this as a partner‑led token.

Element‑by‑element finding
- Existence/identity: Yes. A specific fungible token—Polymarket USD (pUSD)—with a stable ticker and canonical address 0xC011a7E12a19f7B1f670d46F03B03f3342E82DFB is identified on Polymarket’s Contracts page. (Primary Evidence ¶2.)
- Issuer authorization/control: Yes. Polymarket’s own “single source of truth” page lists the token and associates it with Polymarket’s collateral system; that is an issuer‑level attestation. (Primary Evidence ¶2.)
- Official communication before cutoff: Yes. Polymarket’s Changelog and Migration pages publicly communicate that Polymarket shipped pUSD as part of the April 2026 upgrade, with an explicit go‑live time of April 28, 2026 11:00 UTC (04:00 PT). (Primary Evidence ¶¶3–4.)
- Nature/scope: Yes. pUSD is a platform‑wide, transferable ERC‑20 serving as the standard collateral token for all trading. (Primary Evidence ¶1.)
- Independence from exclusions: Yes. It is not an NFT, not a promo, not points, and not a partner/community coin. (Primary Evidence ¶1; ¶¶3–4.)

Anticipated defenses and responses
- “Collateral is not the platform’s token.” The proposition does not require a governance or reward token—only an “official fungible token” launched and communicated by Polymarket. pUSD is the platform’s canonical collateral and is expressly described and deployed by Polymarket. That satisfies “official fungible token” under the plain language, and it operates across the platform rather than within single markets.
- “It’s just a wrapper/points.” The record characterizes pUSD as a standard ERC‑20 backed by USDC and used for all trading; it is transferable and not a non‑transferable points balance. The exclusions thus do not apply.
- “Timing ambiguity.” The official Changelog provides the go‑live date and time in UTC; converting to Pacific confirms April 28, 2026 04:00 PT, many months before the January 1, 2027 00:00 PST deadline.

Conclusion
Taking Polymarket’s own announcements and contract listing together with on‑chain corroboration, it is more likely than not that, well before January 1, 2027 00:00 PST, Polymarket launched an official fungible token—pUSD—and publicly communicated that launch via its official documentation site. The proposition should be found TRUE.

Record/use note
The official‑source and primary‑evidence summaries in this filing are already in the record; no new external materials are introduced here. If the Council prefers immutable archives or server‑timestamped headers, we can supplement via technical report, but the present record suffices under preponderance.

offered_files.label: DX-1 Unresolved record gaps noted in the case record

payload.text:
Council,

Issue and burden
The proposition requires you to find, by a preponderance, that before January 1, 2027 at 00:00 PST, Polymarket (i) launched an official fungible token and (ii) publicly communicated that launch through its own official channels, with the enumerated exclusions enforced. Ambiguity on any required element defeats the claim under this standard.

What the current record shows (and does not)
- Conceded from the record: Polymarket’s docs describe “pUSD” as a standard ERC‑20 on Polygon used as exchange collateral, and their Contracts page lists a CollateralToken proxy address for pUSD. The docs’ changelog and V2 migration materials (as captured) frame pUSD as part of a broader CLOB V2 rollout occurring on April 28, 2026 (11:00 UTC ≈ 04:00 PT). See official‑source and primary‑evidence summaries in PX‑1 and PX‑2.
- Also in the record: the captures were made post‑run; server‑dated headers, page publication/change histories, and independent archives are not preserved. The record itself flags that a later adjudicator could distinguish an “official token launch” from a “technical collateral token introduced as part of CLOB V2.” See “unresolved‑record‑gaps.txt.”

Why this record does not satisfy the proposition
1) “Launched an official fungible token” vs. “shipped a collateral module.
   The only Polymarket communications in the record present pUSD as an internal collateral instrument within an exchange upgrade. They do not announce a standalone token launch (no distribution program, no token economics, no external listing or launch framing). Shipping a collateral contract that mints a 1:1 USDC‑backed wrapper for settlement is not the same thing as launching a Polymarket token in the ordinary sense contemplated by the proposition. The plaintiff’s theory conflates “code deployment that emits an ERC‑20” with “token launch.” The proposition’s operative verb—“launched”—does meaningful work.

2) “Official fungible token” demands issuer‑level release, not mere inclusion among contracts.
   The Contracts page being a “single source of truth” for addresses evidences code lineage, not that Polymarket “released” pUSD as its official platform token. The record contains no statement that Polymarket is the issuer of a new asset versus an operator of a wrapper that tokenizes deposited USDC for internal use. That distinction matters: a deposit receipt used to normalize decimals and collateral flow is a technical artifact, not a native token launch.

3) Public communication of the launch is not shown.
   Even accepting that the changelog/migration pages were public on April 28, 2026, what they communicate is a system upgrade that includes a collateral token—not that Polymarket launched a token. The communication content is technical and incremental. Under the proposition, the plaintiff must show that the launch of the token itself was publicly communicated as such by Polymarket. The record stops short of that.

4) Exclusions and purpose cut against treating pUSD as the qualifying token.
   The exclusions remove points, promos, NFTs, and partner/community coins. pUSD is none of those, but it is best read as a custodial/contract wrapper for USDC used purely as trading collateral. Treating such wrappers as “official token launches” would collapse the intended distinction and render the proposition trivially satisfied by any internal ERC‑20 issued for plumbing. The better reading—consistent with the proposition’s framing and the market’s ordinary usage—is that an “official fungible token” is a native Polymarket asset released as such, not an internal collateral receipt.

5) Evidence integrity and timing safeguards are thin.
   The plaintiff’s exhibits are post‑run captures without immutable timestamps or server headers. For a timing‑sensitive proposition with a January 1, 2027 cutoff, the absence of archival proofs and precise publication histories leaves open whether the specific statements relied on were present as framed at the asserted times. This is not to say they were not; it is to say the plaintiff bears the risk of that uncertainty.

What we rely on
- Only the visible case file: PX‑1 (official‑source record), PX‑2 (primary‑evidence summary), and the noted “unresolved‑record‑gaps.txt.” We introduce no outside material and make no claims beyond the record.

Conclusion
Under a fair reading of the proposition, “launching an official fungible token” requires more than deploying a collateral wrapper contract and mentioning it in technical docs. The record shows a CLOB V2 upgrade that employs pUSD as collateral; it does not make it more likely than not that Polymarket launched—and publicly communicated the launch of—an official Polymarket token within the meaning of the proposition. The plaintiff has not carried its burden. The proposition should be found NOT PROVEN.

Council,

The defense’s objections add requirements the proposition does not contain and that the record does not support.

1) “Launch” does not require tokenomics, listings, or a standalone campaign.
The proposition asks whether, before Jan 1, 2027 at 00:00 PST, Polymarket launched an official fungible token and publicly communicated that launch, subject to explicit exclusions. It does not require governance features, incentives, external exchange listings, or a distribution plan. Polymarket’s own docs state they shipped “a new collateral token (pUSD)” and that “CLOB V2 is live on production” on April 28, 2026, with a migration guide specifically about pUSD. See PX‑1 (changelog; v2 migration) and PX‑2. Rolling out a new ERC‑20 used platform‑wide and instructing users to migrate is a launch in the ordinary sense: Polymarket made the token available for its intended production use and announced it.

2) “Official fungible token” is met by issuer‑level attribution and platform scope.
Polymarket’s Contracts page expressly calls itself the “single source of truth” and lists “pUSD — CollateralToken (proxy)” at a specific address. The pUSD concept page describes pUSD as the collateral token used for all trading, standard ERC‑20, transferable, with 6 decimals, backed by USDC. Those are on‑domain, issuer‑level statements identifying a fungible token integral to the platform, not a per‑market outcome share. PX‑1; PX‑2. That is precisely “official” and “fungible.”

3) Public communication and timing are shown on Polymarket’s own channels, pre‑cutoff.
The April 17, 2026 changelog entry announces the “new collateral token (pUSD)” and sets go‑live for April 28, 2026 ~11:00 UTC; the April 28 entry confirms “CLOB V2 is live” and links the migration guide. Converting 11:00 UTC on April 28, 2026 yields 04:00 Pacific Time on April 28, 2026—many months before January 1, 2027 at 00:00 PST. PX‑1; PX‑2. That is a public communication of the token’s launch on an official channel, with explicit timing.

4) The exclusions were drafted, and satisfied, to avoid false positives—not to carve out collateral.
The proposition excludes NFTs, promos, tokenized points, and partner/community coins. pUSD is none of these. Calling it a “wrapper” does not change that it is a transferable ERC‑20 Polymarket declared and deployed for platform‑wide use. If the framers intended to exclude collateral/wrappers, they would have said so. They did not. Under the text actually adopted, pUSD qualifies.

5) Evidence integrity under a preponderance standard.
The defense concedes the substance of the Polymarket pages and their dates as captured. The pages are official, internally consistent, cross‑referenced, and matched by on‑chain/explorer corroboration (secondary). PX‑1; PX‑2. Preponderance does not demand immutable archives where the issuer’s own contemporaneous materials and the contract listing align. On this record, the more‑likely‑than‑not finding plainly favors the plaintiff.

Bottom line
All elements are met: identity and fungibility (pUSD), official authorization and contract (Contracts page), public communication (docs changelog/migration), timing (April 28, 2026 04:00 PT), and exclusions (not NFTs/promos/points/partner). The defense’s theory narrows “launch” beyond the proposition’s text. The proposition should be found TRUE.

Council,

Even crediting PX‑1 and PX‑2 exactly as the plaintiff states, the record still does not make it more likely than not that Polymarket “launched an official fungible token” and “publicly communicated that launch.” The strongest points in rebuttal—(a) the changelog/migration pages naming “a new collateral token (pUSD)” with an April 28, 2026 go‑live and (b) the Contracts page listing a proxy address—do not carry the specific “launch and public communication of the launch” element.

1) What the official pages actually communicate. The April 2026 entries are framed as a coordinated system upgrade (new exchange contracts, rewritten CLOB backend) that includes a collateral token for settlement. They are not framed as a discrete token launch. There is no page or post presenting “the launch of a Polymarket token” as such; rather, there is a technical migration to new collateral within a backend roll‑out. Accepting that the upgrade went live on April 28, 2026 does not answer the dispositive point: the communications describe plumbing, not a token launch. Under the proposition, “launched … and publicly communicated that launch” does independent work beyond “went live with an internal module.”

2) “Official fungible token” versus internal wrapper. The record characterizes pUSD as a standard ERC‑20 “backed by USDC” used as collateral. That is fully consistent with a custodial wrapper/receipt for settlement—an internal instrument that normalizes collateral—not a native platform token “released” to the public in the sense the proposition contemplates. If every ERC‑20 used for internal accounting counted, the proposition would be satisfied by any plumbing token. The exclusions (points/promos/NFTs/partner‑coins) show the drafters intended to avoid false positives; reading “official fungible token” to include a collateral wrapper collapses that distinction. The plaintiff’s rebuttal does not bridge this gap; it simply asserts that being transferable and platform‑wide is enough. The text requires more: an official token launch, not just an ERC‑20 present in the stack.

3) Contracts registry ≠ launch announcement. The “single source of truth” contracts page evidences address lineage, not that Polymarket “released” a token or that it “publicly communicated the launch” of that token. Listing a contract address is not the same as publicly announcing a launch of a token as a product/asset.

They do not communicate that Polymarket launched a token; they communicate that an exchange upgrade employing a collateral token is live.

PX‑1 and PX‑2 show Polymarket’s own “pUSD (Polymarket USD)” concept page describing pUSD as “the collateral token used for all trading on Polymarket,” a standard ERC‑20 on Polygon, transferable, with six decimals, backed by USDC. That is a fungible cryptoasset operating platform‑wide—not a per‑market share. (PX‑1, PX‑2.)

Polymarket’s Contracts page, which it designates as the “single source of truth,” lists “pUSD — CollateralToken (proxy)” at 0xC011a7E12a19f7B1f670d46F03B03f3342E82DFB. That on‑domain attribution ties the token and its proxy lineage directly to Polymarket. Polygonscan displays the same address as “Polymarket: pUSD Token,” corroborating production reality. (PX‑1, PX‑2.)

3) Launch and public communication before the cutoff (when and how it was launched)
Polymarket’s own Changelog states it was “shipping … a new collateral token (pUSD)” with exchange upgrades “go live April 28, 2026 ~11:00 UTC,” and on April 28, 2026 confirms “CLOB V2 is live on production,” pointing users to the migration guide for pUSD. The V2 Migration page says Polymarket “has shipped” the upgrade “and a new collateral token (Polymarket USD, or pUSD).” 11:00 UTC on April 28, 2026 is 04:00 Pacific Time on April 28, 2026—well before 00:00 PST on January 1, 2027. These are public, official‑channel communications that the token went live as part of the production rollout. (PX‑1, PX‑2.)

4) Exclusions do not apply (what it is not)
The same pages establish pUSD is not an NFT, not a promotional coupon, not a tokenized points system, and not a partner/community coin. It is a transferable ERC‑20 used platform‑wide as collateral and authored by Polymarket. (PX‑1, PX‑2.)

The defense argues that describing pUSD within a system upgrade is “plumbing,” not a “token launch,” and that “launch” must mean a standalone campaign with tokenomics or listings. That is exactly what the Changelog and Migration pages do, and the Contracts page supplies the canonical address. Recasting that coordinated, dated go‑live as “not a launch” adds words the proposition does not contain.

On timing proof, the record presents two consistent, dated official pages (changelog and migration) cross‑referencing the same event and token, with explorer corroboration and a contracts registry that Polymarket itself calls authoritative. Under preponderance, that alignment is sufficient. The defense identifies no contrary official statement or alternative timeline.

Bottom line
The record establishes: (a) a specific fungible token—pUSD—identified by name and contract on Polymarket’s own “single source of truth”; (b) Polymarket’s on‑domain statements that it shipped this new token as part of the April 28, 2026 production rollout (11:00 UTC = 04:00 PT); and (c) that none of the exclusions apply. On this evidence, it is more likely than not that, before January 1, 2027 at 00:00 PST, Polymarket launched an official fungible token and publicly communicated that launch. The existing record meets each element.

1) Identity and fungibility (what the token is)
PX‑1 and PX‑2 show Polymarket’s own “pUSD (Polymarket USD)” concept page describing pUSD as “the collateral token used for all trading on Polymarket,” a standard ERC‑20 on Polygon, transferable, with six decimals, backed by USDC. That is a fungible cryptoasset operating platform‑wide—not a per‑market share. (PX‑1, PX‑2.)

2) Official authorization and canonical address (who issued it)
Polymarket’s Contracts page, which it designates as the “single source of truth,” lists “pUSD — CollateralToken (proxy)” at 0xC011a7E12a19f7B1f670d46F03B03f3342E82DFB. That on‑domain attribution ties the token and its proxy lineage directly to Polymarket. Polygonscan displays the same address as “Polymarket: pUSD Token,” corroborating production reality. (PX‑1, PX‑2.)

3) Launch and public communication before the cutoff (when and how it was launched)
Polymarket’s own Changelog states it was “shipping … a new collateral token (pUSD)” with exchange upgrades “go live April 28, 2026 ~11:00 UTC,” and on April 28, 2026 confirms “CLOB V2 is live on production,” pointing users to the migration guide for pUSD. The V2 Migration page says Polymarket “has shipped” the upgrade “and a new collateral token (Polymarket USD, or pUSD).” 11:00 UTC on April 28, 2026 is 04:00 Pacific Time on April 28, 2026—well before 00:00 PST on January 1, 2027. These are public, official‑channel communications that the token went live as part of the production rollout. (PX‑1, PX‑2.)

4) Exclusions do not apply (what it is not)
The same pages establish pUSD is not an NFT, not a promotional coupon, not a tokenized points system, and not a partner/community coin. It is a transferable ERC‑20 used platform‑wide as collateral and authored by Polymarket. (PX‑1, PX‑2.)

Addressing the defense’s best point
The defense argues that describing pUSD within a system upgrade is “plumbing,” not a “token launch,” and that “launch” must mean a standalone campaign with tokenomics or listings. The proposition contains no such requirements. “Launched an official fungible token” is met when Polymarket made a new ERC‑20 (pUSD) available for its intended production use and told users—on official channels—that this new token was part of the live system and provided a migration path. That is exactly what the Changelog and Migration pages do, and the Contracts page supplies the canonical address. Recasting that coordinated, dated go‑live as “not a launch” adds words the proposition does not contain.

On timing proof, the record presents two consistent, dated official pages (changelog and migration) cross‑referencing the same event and token, with explorer corroboration and a contracts registry that Polymarket itself calls authoritative. Under preponderance, that alignment is sufficient. The defense identifies no contrary official statement or alternative timeline.

Bottom line
The record establishes: (a) a specific fungible token—pUSD—identified by name and contract on Polymarket’s own “single source of truth”; (b) Polymarket’s on‑domain statements that it shipped this new token as part of the April 28, 2026 production rollout (11:00 UTC = 04:00 PT); and (c) that none of the exclusions apply. On this evidence, it is more likely than not that, before January 1, 2027 at 00:00 PST, Polymarket launched an official fungible token and publicly communicated that launch. The proposition should be found TRUE.

Council,

Under this forum’s standard, the plaintiff must make it more likely than not that, before January 1, 2027 at 00:00 PST, Polymarket (i) launched an official fungible token and (ii) publicly communicated that launch on its own channels, with the stated exclusions enforced. What the record actually establishes

- The Contracts page—styled the “single source of truth”—lists “pUSD — CollateralToken (proxy)” at a specific address. (PX‑1; PX‑2.)
- The Changelog and V2 Migration pages present a coordinated system upgrade (CLOB V2) and state that a “new collateral token (pUSD)” is part of that rollout, with production go‑live on April 28, 2026 around 11:00 UTC (≈ 04:00 Pacific). (PX‑1; PX‑2.)
- Polygonscan reflects a token at the same address; this is corroborative only. (PX‑1; PX‑2.)

Why this does not carry the plaintiff’s burden
1) Launch of a token versus deployment of collateral plumbing. The operative verb is “launched.” The only on‑domain communications in the record frame pUSD as an internal collateral instrument within an exchange upgrade. They do not present a discrete token launch. There is no official announcement that Polymarket has launched a Polymarket token as such; there is a migration to a collateral wrapper used for settlement. Treating any ERC‑20 used for internal accounting as a “token launch” would collapse the proposition.

2) “Official fungible token” requires an issuer‑level release, not merely inclusion in a contracts registry. The Contracts page proves address lineage; it is not itself a launch announcement. Nor do the materials say Polymarket “released” or “launched” a native platform token. The record’s description of pUSD as USDC‑backed collateral is fully consistent with a custodial wrapper/receipt—not a native token issued to the public in the sense contemplated by the proposition.

3) Public communication of the launch is not shown. Even if one accepts the April 28, 2026 timing, the content of the communications matters. The Changelog/Migration pages communicate that CLOB V2 is live and uses pUSD; they do not communicate that Polymarket launched a token. The requirement is “publicly communicated that launch,” i.e., a communication about the launch of the token itself. The record stops short of that.

4) The exclusions reinforce the intended boundary. The proposition excludes NFTs, promotions, tokenized point systems, and partner/community coins—signals that the framers were cabining “official token” to a native Polymarket asset released as such. A USDC‑backed collateral receipt used for settlement is a different category; counting it would render the exclusions toothless and make the proposition trivially satisfied by back‑end plumbing.

5) Timing proofs do not cure the content gap. DX‑1 notes the absence of immutable timestamps and server headers. But the decisive defect is not timing; it is what the pages say. Even crediting the dates and the go‑live conversion, the communications still describe an exchange upgrade that employs a collateral token—not a token launch.

The best plaintiff evidence is the April 2026 language that Polymarket was “shipping … a new collateral token (pUSD)” and that “CLOB V2 is live on production,” plus the Contracts entry. Under a preponderance standard, where two reasonable readings remain—(a) internal collateral wrapper shipped with an upgrade (the record’s plain framing) and (b) an official token launch—the tie goes to not proven.

On this record, it is proven that Polymarket’s CLOB V2 went live on April 28, 2026 and that it employs pUSD, an ERC‑20 collateral token listed on Polymarket’s contracts page. It is not proven that Polymarket launched—and publicly communicated the launch of—an official Polymarket fungible token within the meaning of the proposition. The evidence is at best equivocal on the critical “launch and public communication of the launch” element. Under preponderance, that is insufficient. The proposition should be found NOT PROVEN.

payload.rationale: The evidence presented shows that Polymarket introduced pUSD and communicated its deployment in official channels as a collateral token. However, the documentation primarily treats pUSD as part of an infrastructure update, not a standalone platform token launch. According to the proposition, introducing backend functionalities in this manner does not equate to a new official fungible token launch. Therefore, the claim that Polymarket launched an official fungible token, as defined, is not proven by the preponderance of the evidence.

Polymarket's own Contracts page lists pUSD as an ERC‑20 collateral token, its changelog and migration pages show a coordinated April 28, 2026 go‑live of the pUSD collateral module (~04:00 PT), and pUSD is described as a transferable, platform‑wide fungible asset. Those official communications and on‑chain attribution meet the proposition’s terms, making it more likely than not that Polymarket launched and publicly announced an official fungible token before the January 1, 2027 cutoff.

Polymarket's official documentation identifies pUSD as a fungible ERC-20 collateral token, explicitly lists its contract address, and communicates its live deployment before the deadline. While part of a larger system upgrade, this constitutes the launch and public communication of an official fungible token under a preponderance standard. The exclusions for NFTs, promotions, and points do not apply.

Polymarket's own official docs — the Contracts page, Changelog, and V2 Migration guide — put it in writing that they shipped pUSD, a standard ERC-20, as part of their April 28, 2026 production rollout, well before the January 1, 2027 cutoff. When a business makes a written, on-domain statement identifying their own token by name, address, and go-live date, I treat that as proof of what they did — not ambiguous plumbing. The defense's argument that a collateral wrapper isn't a "launched token" adds requirements the proposition simply does not contain, and I'm not going to reward that kind of hairsplitting.
The proposition is not proven as the evidence provided does not clearly show that Polymarket launched and publicly communicated the launch of an official fungible token.
